items.back: 1. Other approved separation assured BEFORE and AFTER the visual separation. 2. Weather allows aircraft to remain in sight until other separation exists. 3. Lead aircraft is NOT a super. Consider: aircraft performance, wake turbulence, closure rate, routes of flight, known weather, aircraft position.

items.front: Altitude limit for visual separation per 7-2-1
items.back: Up to but not including FL180 for both terminal (a) and en route (b) applications.

items.front: Tower-applied visual separation: when NOT authorized per 7-2-1a1
items.back: NOT authorized when: (d) departure routes/aircraft performance preclude maintaining separation between successive departures, or (e) wake turbulence separation is required. Also never when lead is a super (body).

items.front: Pilot-applied visual separation: three steps per 7-2-1a2(b)
items.back: 1. Tell pilot about other aircraft (position, direction, type, and intention unless obvious). 2. Obtain acknowledgment that aircraft is in sight. 3. Instruct pilot to MAINTAIN VISUAL SEPARATION.

items.front: APPROVED shortcut for visual separation per 7-2-1
items.back: If pilot reports traffic in sight AND will maintain visual separation (must state BOTH), controller may say '(ACID), APPROVED' instead of restating the instruction.

items.front: Targets merging advisory: terminal vs en route per 7-2-1
items.back: Terminal: 'Targets appear likely to merge.' En route: 'Radar targets appear likely to merge.' Issue when giving visual sep instruction, when advising other aircraft of converging course, or later if merging becomes apparent.

Tower must apply a1 or a2 procedures; states to radar controller it will provide visual separation with call signs of all aircraft. Radar controller approves with 'VISUAL SEPARATION APPROVED, (ACIDs) RELEASED.' IFR separation before/after remains Approach/Departure/En Route responsibility.

items.front: En route visual separation additional requirements per 7-2-1b
items.back: Beyond pilot-applied basics: (e) inform other aircraft of converging course, (f) advise if radar targets appear likely to merge, (g) advise if either aircraft is a HEAVY, (h) issue wake turbulence cautionary advisories per 2-1-20.
